EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front blasted through the western Carolinas during the morning of the 6th, bringing gusty winds and the coldest air mass to have affected the region since 1994. By early evening, winds of 10 to 20 mph, with stronger gusts combined with temperatures falling into the 20s and teens to produce wind chill values below 0 across the South Carolina mountains. Wind chills across the higher elevations likely reached -15 or lower briefly on the night of the 6th. Although wind gradually diminished overnight, low temperatures on the 7th ranged from a little below zero above 3000 feet to the single digits across the Piedmont and foothills.
Strong and moist southerly flow produced strong thunderstorms with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a strong thunderstorm produce flooding and a subsequent road closure on Tema Road between Bay Road and High Point Road.

An arctic cold front blasted through the western Carolinas during the morning of the 6th, bringing gusty winds and the coldest air mass to have affected the region since 1994. By early evening, winds of 10 to 20 mph, with stronger gusts combined with temperatures falling into the 20s and teens to produce wind chill values below 0 across the South Carolina mountains. Wind chills across the higher elevations likely reached -15 or lower briefly on the night of the 6th. Although wind gradually diminished overnight, low temperatures on the 7th ranged from a little below zero above 3000 feet to the single digits across the Piedmont and foothills.

Light snow developed over the Piedmont and foothills of the Western Carolinas during the afternoon and continued into the evening before tapering off. The snow initially melted on roads. However, air temperatures rapidly cooling into the 20s caused many roads to subsequently freeze. Although snowfall totals were light (1 to 2 inches or less in most areas), the slick roads caused hundreds of traffic accidents.

Light snow developed across the mountains during the morning and continued into the afternoon before tapering off. The snow initially melted on roads. However, air temperatures rapidly cooling into the lower and mid 20s caused many roads to subsequently freeze. Although snowfall totals were very light (an inch or less in most areas), the slick roads caused hundreds of traffic accidents.

Tide levels were elevated during morning hours due to a persistent north/northeast flow and astronomical influences.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from social media indicated one car stuck in flood waters on East Bay Street near Harris and Lee Streets. The Charleston Harbor tide gage measured a maximum height of 7.38 feet mean lower low water at 930 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tide levels were elevated during morning hours due to a persistent north/northeast flow and astronomical influences.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets and intersections were closed in downtown Charleston, including a line from Hagood to Fishburne, Market  between Church and State, Ashley Avenue from Race to Fishburne, Bennett between Ashley and Halsey, Bennett and Gadsden, Wentworth and Barre and Ashley between Tradd and Broad. The Charleston tide gage reached a height of 7.31 feet mean lower low water at 10 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The presence of strong wind fields in advance of a cold front approaching from the west led to a period of gusty winds within rain over southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported a tree down near the intersection of Remount Road and North Rhett Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tide levels were elevated during morning hours due to a persistent northerly flow and astronomical influences.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee reported parts of East Bay Street flooded and impassable. A maximum tide level of 7.16 feet mean lower low water was measured at the Charleston Harbor tide gage at 730 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tide levels were elevated during morning hours due to a persistent northeast flow and perigean spring tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report relayed via Twitter indicated that northbound East Bay Street is closed past the bridge.  The Charleston Harbor tide gage measured a maximum level of 7.46 feet mean lower low water at 818 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved up the Georgia/South Carolina coast producing freezing rain, sleet, and snow across the area.  Some trees and powerlines were down causing power outages and traveling throughout the Midlands and CSRA were quite hazardous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced freezing rain, sleet, and snow across Lee County causing hazardous traveling conditions.  Freezing rain accumulations were around 1/8 of an inch with snow/sleet accumulations from 1 to 3 inches across the county.

Freezing rain accumulations were around 1/4 of an inch with snow/sleet accumulations from 1 to 3 inches across the county.  There was one injury when a person slipped and fell down from ice on the walkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of light to moderate precipitation with embedded thunderstorms developed in the morning hours ahead of a strong cold front and pushed into southeast South Carolina in the afternoon. A few of the thunderstorms became strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hampton County warning point reported a tree blown down on Hickory Hill Road in Varnville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed in line segments behind an advancing warm front in the evening hours. A few of the thunderstorms became strong to severe and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Allendale County warning point reported a few trees blown down on Highway 125 between Martin and Appleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed in line segments behind an advancing warm front in the evening hours. A few of the thunderstorms became strong to severe and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a tree blown down onto power lines near the intersection of Midland Park Road and Townsend Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy rain showers and isolated thunderstorms moved from East Central Georgia into South Carolina's lower Piedmont during the evening and produced isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a large pine tree was blown down on a vehicle in the Greenwood area. Highway Patrol reported at least three additional trees blown down across central and eastern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with a strong upper level disturbance and associated strong cold front developed across Upstate South Carolina during the early morning hours. As an arctic air mass infiltrated the region behind the front, the precipitation briefly changed to snow in some areas, particularly along an axis from the Greenville area south to Laurens and Greenwood. Although the snow was moderate to heavy at times, a very warm ground and temperatures around 40 degrees resulted in total accumulation of only a dusting up to maybe a couple of inches on grassy surfaces. However, snow accumulated more heavily on elevated surfaces, including trees and power lines. The combination of very heavy, wet snow accumulating on power lines and trees that were still leafed, along with wind gusts of 30-40 mph brought down many trees and power lines across portions of Greenville, Greenwood, Anderson, and Laurens Counties. What little snow did accumulate melted by noon or so.

Heavy rains over the Midlands of South Carolina produced some locally heavy rains in a couple of hours that produced flash flooding in areas of Columbia.  One to two feet of water covered portions of roads in the Five Points area and also at the intersection of Main and Whaley streets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS WEBCAM showed 1 to 2 feet of water flooding the intersection of Whaley and Main streets from the Rocky Branch Creek flowing across the roadway.  Other roads in the Five Points area also had 1 to 2 feet of water for a short period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air surged into the region, undercutting warmer air aloft.  This coupled with an area of low pressure tracking northeast along the front produced a variety of winter weather with a mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began falling the afternoon of Jan 28th and may have briefly changed over to sleet before tapering off to flurries the morning of the 29th.  Total ice accumulations were about a three tenths of an inch.  Due to the nature of the precipitation, power outages were isolated, however driving was treacherous.  Numerous traffic accidents were reported as well as injuries due to slips and falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm spread into South Carolina producing up to 1.25 inches of ice taking down trees, powerlines, and causing damage to other structures.  Just about everything came to a standstill as federal, state, county, and city governments closed along with schools and businesses.  Power outages were widespread and it took up to four days to restore in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major Winter Storm produced 1/3 inch of ice and 3 to 6 inches of snow and sleet across Kershaw County taking down some trees and powerlines. Several areas reported power outages which were mainly confined to the southern half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm and humid air was in place ahead of a strong cold front. The environment was characterized as high shear and modest CAPE, up to 1500 J/kg. Line segments did move into or form across the forecast area during the mid and late afternoon. Some of these segments briefly bowed out, producing strong to damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on Interstate 95 southbound at mile marker 184. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Gulf of Mexico, bringing abundant moisture from both the Gulf and the Atlantic Ocean. Strong ascent aided in precipitation development and High astronomical tides made conditions ideal for flooding in Downtown Charleston. Many locations reported 2 to 3 inches of rain accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rain and high astronomical tides led to flash flooding in Downtown Charleston. Streets included: Calhoun, Ashley, Rutledge, Bennett, Gadsden, Ogier, Vanderhourst, Spring, President, Market and East Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was under a mid/upper level northwest flow as the main ridge axis was to our west. At the surface, there was a stalled frontal boundary aligning west to east across the central Midlands. The atmosphere became moderately unstable with some indices in the strong category. The K Index and Cape were in the strong category. The wind field was relatively weak, but there was some mid level dry air that may enhance thunderstorm downdrafts leading to strong damaging winds. PW values were around 1.75 inches. This combined with slow storm movement could potentially produce locally heavy rain and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 Tornado snapped off a few trees and uprooted a couple of trees along a path from near Ditch Bank Road southeast across Old School House Road then across Ebenezer Road to near Fiesta Court. The estimated wind speeds ranged from 75 to 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough extended across central and eastern portions of the Carolinas. Convection developed along and east of the trough as instability from heating peaked. Sea breeze convection also fired during the later afternoon. By early evening, outflow boundaries interacted, resulting in a couple severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size was reported on Minturn Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed over the Piedmont and foothills of the Western Carolinas during the afternoon and continued into the evening before tapering off. The snow initially melted on roads. However, air temperatures rapidly cooling into the 20s caused many roads to subsequently freeze. Although snowfall totals were light (1 to 2 inches or less in most areas), the slick roads caused hundreds of traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed over the Piedmont and foothills of the Western Carolinas during the afternoon and continued into the evening before tapering off. The snow initially melted on roads. However, air temperatures rapidly cooling into the 20s caused many roads to subsequently freeze. Although snowfall totals were light (1 to 2 inches or less in most areas), the slick roads caused hundreds of traffic accidents.

Scattered thunderstorms developed over the mountains and foothills during the afternoon and moved east/southeast into the Piedmont. A couple of brief severe storms  occurred across the northern Upstate and the eastern Piedmont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small area of heavy rain developed across the Upstate during the pre-dawn hours and remained nearly stationary for several hours. By late morning, 4-7 inches had fallen along a narrow axis from eastern Anderson County through southern and central Greenville County. The heaviest rain fell across eastern and southern portions of the Greenville metro area, where some flash flooding developed before the rain tapered off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported flash flooding developed along a low portion of Richland Creek near downtown Greenville after 4 to 7 inches of rain fell in the hour over about a 6 hour period. The main impact was to Cleveland Park, which had to be closed for a while due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled across the area. As intense heating ensued, several clusters of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon with a few reaching severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A team from the National Weather Service conducted a survey and found a damage path from the intersection of Winnie Road and Old Nichols Highway to near the intersection of Taylor Road and Pace Road. Numerous large tree limbs were blown out to include the tops of two large pine trees. Two large hardwood trees were also uprooted. A few pieces of metal roofing were blown off a horse barn and deposited 100 yards away. Minor shingle and siding damage was also observed at two residences. The path length was about three-quarters of a mile and the maximum path width was 350 yards. The damage pattern was consistent with straight line winds up to 75 mph. Time of event was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A depression formed off the Florida coast on June 30th and strengthened as it moved north. The tropical system became a hurricane off the Georgia coast and was named Arthur. Hurricane Arthur continued to move north northeast and further strengthened to a Category 2 hurricane with 100 mph winds as it made its closest pass to Wilmington, North Carolina the evening of July 3rd (40 miles). Tropical storm force winds were felt over much of northeast South Carolina and southeast North Carolina. Heavy rain was also a factor, with many locations receiving over three inches of rainfall. The minimum pressure over land was 996 mb, recorded at Carolina Beach. The hurricane exited the region in the early morning of July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Arthur moved up the eastern seaboard and became a Category 2 hurricane as it passed 40 miles to the east of Wilmington, North Carolina. The strongest wind recorded in the county was 42 mph, measured at North Myrtle Beach. Near tropical storm force winds were experienced for about 8 hours, with sporatic power outages reported. Heavy rain was also a factor with rainfall amounts ranging between one to over two inches. Beach erosion was minimal. The storm exited the region in the early morning of July 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front moving from the northeast toward the southwest caused numerous thunderstorms and heavy rain showers to suddenly erupt as it moved across northern portions of Upstate South Carolina during the evening. The storms moved slowly, while multiple storms moved over some of the same areas repeatedly. The result was extreme rainfall rates that produced 3 to 6 inches of rain in only a couple of hours. Although multiple localized flash flood events occurred across the Upstate, the most severe flooding was in the Greenville metro area, especially near downtown Greenville (where two fatalities occurred) and in the Greer/Taylors area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although the torrential rainfall ended across the Greenville area by midnight, flooding continued in the Reedy River basin near downtown through the night. The stream gauge near Westfield St did not fall below the established flood stage until late morning on the 11th. The main impact was to Cleveland Park, which remained closed due to damage for two days after the flood water receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front combined with a strongly unstable air mass to produce a large number of large hail reports. There were also several wind damage reports with the worst of the damage around Conway, South Carolina. The severe weather was largely clustered in Horry County, South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A team from the National Weather Service conducted a survey and determined a microburst with winds up to 75 mph began between Juniper Bay Road and 4 Mile Road. Damage in this area included scattered tree damage. A nearby trailer park along Sleepy Hollow Lane suffered primarily minor damage to a couple of structures. A tree in the mobile park was uprooted and fell onto a mobile home. There were several large trees uprooted in this area. As the microburst moved to the southeast it caused significant damage. A poorly secured metal roof of a mobile home on Singing Pines Drive was completely lifted from the home. Several large hardwoods were uprooted along Singing Pines Drive and nearby Old Railroad Road. Power lines were also reported down. The microburst weakened, but severe winds still uprooted several trees and downed large limbs along Wayside Road, Juniper Bay Road and Dunn Short Cut Road. The ground was very saturated and almost all of the uprooted trees were hardwoods. The maximum path width was 1.8 miles and the path length was 2.35 miles. Six people were sent to the hospital with minor injuries and quickly released.

Although multiple localized flash flood events occurred across the Upstate, the most severe flooding was in the Greenville metro area, especially near downtown Greenville (where two fatalities occurred) and in the Greer/Taylors area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain ended by around midnight, excessive runoff flowing from Brushy Creek and other tributaries caused flooding to develop along the Enoree River during the early morning hours, continuing until late morning. Multiple roads were flooded along the river between Hammet Bridge Rd and Highway 14. This was the worst flooding along the Enoree since 2005, and among the worst since Tropical Storm Jerry in 1995.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front moving from the northeast toward the southwest caused numerous thunderstorms and heavy rain showers to suddenly erupt as it moved across northern portions of Upstate South Carolina during the evening. The storms moved slowly, while multiple storms moved over some of the same areas repeatedly. The result was extreme rainfall rates that produced 3 to 6 inches of rain in only a couple of hours. Although multiple localized flash flood events occurred across the Upstate, the most severe flooding was in the Greenville metro area, especially near downtown Greenville (where two fatalities occurred) and in the Greer/Taylors area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of rain falling in a couple of hours caused an extreme urban flash flood and stream flooding event in the Greenville metro area. The flooding initially began on the east side of the city in the Haywood Rd/downtown airport area, where several cars were stalled in deep, rushing water due to an overflowing culvert on Byrdland Dr (3 E Downtown). A 39-year-old man and 36-year-old woman who abandoned their vehicles to seek higher ground drowned after being swept into the fast-moving water and nearby culvert which drained into Laurel Creek. A tributary of Richland Creek flooded several dozen apartment homes along Villa Rd between Pleasantburg Dr and Pelham Rd (2.5 ENE Downtown). The most severe flooding occurred on the east side of downtown, near the Stone Ave/I-385 intersection, due to a combination of Richland Creek overflowing its banks by several feet and major urban flooding. Multiple vehicles were stalled in high water in this area. The high water caused a gas leak and explosion at a restaurant on Stone Ave, causing significant damage to the exterior of the building. Richland Creek and the Reedy River overflowed their banks and flooded much of Cleveland Park, which remained closed for two days. This was the worst flooding along this portion of the Reedy in almost 10 years. Additionally, a small tributary of Langston Creek flooded the intersection of Langston Dr and Highway 276 (3 NNW Downtown). Finally, Rocky Creek, or a culvert draining into Rocky Creek overflowed along I-85 near the Pelham Rd exit (7 E Downtown), sending water over the southbound lane and forcing closure of the interstate for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong to severe thunderstorms developed near a stationary front positioned over southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported numerous road closures in downtown Charleston due to flash flooding, including Bull, Calhoun, Ashley Avenue, Fishburne, Morris, Huger, King, Rutledge, Sheppard, Cannon Park, Thomas, Warren and the Crosstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front moving from the northeast toward the southwest caused numerous thunderstorms and heavy rain showers to suddenly erupt as it moved across northern portions of Upstate South Carolina during the evening. The storms moved slowly, while multiple storms moved over some of the same areas repeatedly. The result was extreme rainfall rates that produced 3 to 6 inches of rain in only a couple of hours. Although multiple localized flash flood events occurred across the Upstate, the most severe flooding was in the Greenville metro area, especially near downtown Greenville (where two fatalities occurred) and in the Greer/Taylors area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major flash flood event developed in the Greer and Taylors area when 3 to 6 inches of rain fell in only a couple of hours. The primary problem area was Brushy Creek, which produced its worst flooding in recent history. A stream gauge on Brushy Creek between Hudson Rd and East North St reached its highest level (13.4 ft) since August 1995 (Tropical Storm Jerry). Water entered multiple residences along Edwards Rd in Taylors. Brushy Creek also flooded portions of Boiling Springs Rd and adjacent side streets. In addition, a small tributary washed out a bridge on Memorial Dr Ext near Tryon St (1.5 NW Greer). Two vehicles drove into the resulting hole and each driver received minor injuries. Other small tributaries overflowed their banks throughout the Greer and Taylors area, flooding roads and some homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked from the Gulf of Mexico to off the southeast coast, producing widespread precipitation across southeast South Carolina. A wedge of high pressure provided shallow, cold air, supporting the development of freezing rain at many locations away from the immediate coast. Across many inland areas, a major ice storm occurred with one to three quarters of an inch of ice accumulation and even a few one inch accumulation amounts reported. Widespread trees, large tree limbs, and power lines were reported down due to ice. Many trees were down and blocking area roadways including Interstate 95 and Interstate 26. Widespread, long duration power outages also occurred especially across inland areas. Numerous bridges around the region were closed due to ice accumulation, adding to hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee reported several large tree limbs down due to ice along Clements Ferry Road.

Many trees and power lines were brought down on area roadways including Interstate 26 and Interstate 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying cyclone off the Southeast coast and cold air damming combined to produce a period of rain, sleet, and snow across portions of the South Carolina mountains. Precipitation began during the late evening of the 6th. Precipitation changed to rain in most areas after midnight, but not before 1-2 inches of snow had accumulated across the higher elevations. Rain changed briefly back to heavy snow around daybreak of the 7th, and a narrow corridor along the Blue Ridge saw 1-4 inch storm totals.
Severe storms over areas of the northern Pee Dee region produced baseball size hail.  Other areas of the Midlands produced wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported power lines down across Hardscrabble road between Kelly Mill and Langford roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous strong to severe, discrete thunderstorm cells developed across the Piedmont during the afternoon ahead of a slow-moving, nearly stationary backdoor cold front. The storms were prolific hail producers, with some producing larger than baseball size hail. New cells often developed upstream of mature cells, then moved over the same areas, such that some locations saw two or more rounds of large hail. This was especially true across portions of Spartanburg, Cherokee, Union, and York Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ham radio operator reported dime to quarter size hail at the intersection of Highway 80 and Wade Hampton Blvd.

A 20 year old male was pulled from the water near the 21st Street access point by four Good Samaritans, but died soon after.  CPR was attempted by a fire and rescue unit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong but shallow arctic cold front pushed through the region early in the day on 1/28/14. By that evening, a strong cold air damming regime prevailed as northerly surface winds pushed temperatures to around freezing across nearly all of southeast South Carolina. Above the shallow freezing layer, a prominent elevated warm layer developed which resulted in the predominant precipitation type being freezing rain for the event. Strong synoptic forcing produced widespread precipitation through the event. Storm total ice accumulations ranged from one quarter to three quarters of an inch, especially across far inland areas. There were even a few isolated one inch amounts reported. Total snow and sleet accumulations were less than an inch. The unusually high amounts of ice accretion caused widespread major travel impacts with several bridge being closed due to ice. Furthermore, temperatures remained near or below freezing at many locations through 1/31/14, which slowed the melting of ice and prolonged travel impacts. Also of note, melting ice falling from the towers of the Ravenel Bridge damaged several vehicles and resulted in one non-life threatening injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total ice accumulations ranged up to one half of an inch in the Cross area. There were also trees reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary over northern Florida on February 11th, with a strong and large area of high pressure to the north.  Precipitation began as snow from the I-95 corridor and points west, with mainly sleet and freezing rain reported east of I-95.  The northwest portion of the region received over five inches of snow. Over the course of a couple of days, the storm transitioned to mainly an ice event, with nearly an inch of ice reported over interior portions of northeast South Carolina, with lesser amounts over southeast North Carolina.  In addition to creating extremely hazardous driving conditions, major power outages were reported due to downed limbs and power lines.  Low pressure finally moved up the eastern seaboard and out of the region during the day on February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling the morning of February 11th. After a lull for about 24 hours...more snow fell before ending as freezing rain the morning of February 13th. Snowfall amounts were estimated around two inches, and ice accumulations ranged from  a quarter inch to around an inch.  Driving conditions were treacherous, and there were numerous power outages due to downed limbs and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary over northern Florida on February 11th, with a strong and large area of high pressure to the north.  Precipitation began as snow from the I-95 corridor and points west, with mainly sleet and freezing rain reported east of I-95.  The northwest portion of the region received over five inches of snow. Over the course of a couple of days, the storm transitioned to mainly an ice event, with nearly an inch of ice reported over interior portions of northeast South Carolina, with lesser amounts over southeast North Carolina.  In addition to creating extremely hazardous driving conditions, major power outages were reported due to downed limbs and power lines.  Low pressure finally moved up the eastern seaboard and out of the region during the day on February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling the morning of February 11th.  After a lull for about 24 hours...more snow fell before ending as freezing rain the morning of February 13th.  Snowfall amounts ranged from one to three and a half inches will about a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation. A man was found dead on a baseball field on Leesburg Street in Hartsville, not far from his apartment.  An autopsy found that he died from hypothermia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed beginning in the morning hours along a weak lingering front and moved southward through the day. With the lingering front and relaxed pressure gradient in the area, the environment was supportive of waterspouts along the southeast South Carolina coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak and brief tornado moved onto the south-central part of Kiawah Island, possibly starting as a waterspout.  The tornado traveled west about a tenth of a mile before dissipating.  Beach chairs and umbrellas were thrown but there were no apparent injuries or structural damage.  Winds were estimated to range from 55 to 65 mph.  Video of the event was recorded by the public and relayed to the National Weather Service by local television media. The event took place on the beach area nearly right in front of The Sanctuary Hotel and Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked from the Gulf of Mexico to off the southeast coast, producing widespread precipitation across southeast South Carolina. A wedge of high pressure provided shallow, cold air, supporting the development of freezing rain at many locations away from the immediate coast. Across many inland areas, a major ice storm occurred with one to three quarters of an inch of ice accumulation and even a few one inch accumulation amounts reported. Widespread trees, large tree limbs, and power lines were reported down due to ice. Many trees were down and blocking area roadways including Interstate 95 and Interstate 26. Widespread, long duration power outages also occurred especially across inland areas. Numerous bridges around the region were closed due to ice accumulation, adding to hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total ice accumulations across Inland Berkeley County ranged from around one quarter of an inch up to an inch. The lesser amounts were observed closer to the coast near Goose Creek and Huger, while the highest amounts occurred further inland around Cross. Widespread trees and power lines were reported down across the county with widespread, long duration power outages as well. Many trees and large limbs were down on area roadways, including Interstate 26. One indirect fatality and one indirect injury resulted from the ice storm as well.  The fatality and injury occurred  when investigators found that a couple in the Ladson area had been using a charcoal grill to heat their enclosed bedroom in their mobile home. The 25 year old male died from carbon monoxide poisoning and his wife, of unknown age, fell ill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Miller type-A low pressure system moved up along the South Carolina coast bringing widespread snow, which by late morning began to change to sleet and freezing rain.  This transition zone gradually spread north throughout the day and into the evening, although precipitation periodically changed back to snow later in the evening and during the overnight. Snowfall and sleet accumulation generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the area, with locally higher amounts reported in the high elevations. Meanwhile, some areas saw light ice accretion of around a tenth of an inch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began falling the morning of February 11th.  The freezing rain continued intermittently before ending the morning of the 13th.  Ice accumulations ranged from a quarter inch up to an inch.  Driving conditions were treacherous and there were numerous power outages due to falling limbs and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure formed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary over northern Florida on February 11th, with a strong and large area of high pressure to the north.  Precipitation began as snow from the I-95 corridor and points west, with mainly sleet and freezing rain reported east of I-95.  The northwest portion of the region received over five inches of snow. Over the course of a couple of days, the storm transitioned to mainly an ice event, with nearly an inch of ice reported over interior portions of northeast South Carolina, with lesser amounts over southeast North Carolina.  In addition to creating extremely hazardous driving conditions, major power outages were reported due to downed limbs and power lines.  Low pressure finally moved up the eastern seaboard and out of the region during the day on February 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began falling the morning of February 11th. The freezing rain continued intermittently before ending the morning of the 13th.  About an inch of ice accumulation was reported.  Driving was treacherous if not impossible.  Many power outages occurred due to downed power lines and branches from the ice.  A woman was found dead in her home in the town of Marion.  She died of hypothermia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved into the area along a strong cold front near midday. There was some wind damage west of Interstate 95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down across the roadway at the intersection of Leavensworth Road and Dovesville Highway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down near the intersection of Chelsea Lane and Highway 381.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convective cells embedded within an area of stratiform rain developed in association with a strong low level jet. While the cells were generally weak, they were able to generate damaging wind gusts as they tapped the gradient winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flag pole snapped on West Whitner St (2 W Anderson), and a tree was blown down across Highway 81 south of Starr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west provided a tight pressure gradient, producing strong winds with scattered thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported quarter size hail at Fort Moultrie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a weak frontal boundary and an approaching upper level low pressure system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Highway Department reported trees down just outside of Trenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches produced flash flooding in Lancaster and northwest Chesterfield counties washing out several roads and bridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SC Highway Patrol reported road washout on Tabernacle road between Buford High School and Shiloh Unity Road.  Other roads in the area were impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours across southeast Georgia and moved eastward into southeast South Carolina. These thunderstorms developed in a warm and moist atmosphere well ahead of a cold front to the west. Some of the thunderstorms organized into multi-cell clusters and numerous reports of wind damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees reported down in the Yemassee area with an unknown amount of power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours and lingered into the evening across portions of southeast South Carolina. These thunderstorms developed along and just south of a nearly stationary front to the north and produced numerous lightning strikes and thunderstorm wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Carolina Highway Patrol reported a tree down on Glover Road on St. Helena Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours and lingered into the evening across portions of southeast South Carolina. These thunderstorms developed along and just south of a nearly stationary front to the north and produced numerous lightning strikes and thunderstorm wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaufort County communications center reported a tree down on Perry Road at Orange Grove Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As temperatures reached near 100 degrees, isolated strong to severe thunderstorms developed across the central Midlands and CSRA. PW values near 2.0 inches helped to produced locally heavy rainfall with slow moving storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aiken Electric Cooperative reported power outages due to trees on a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmosphere within the vicinity of a stationary front supported excessive rainfall amounts and flash flooding as multi-cell thunderstorms merged over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two feet of water was reported across portions of Rivers Avenue near Interstate 526.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front moving from the northeast toward the southwest caused numerous thunderstorms and heavy rain showers to suddenly erupt as it moved across northern portions of Upstate South Carolina during the evening. The storms moved slowly, while multiple storms moved over some of the same areas repeatedly. The result was extreme rainfall rates that produced 3 to 6 inches of rain in only a couple of hours. Although multiple localized flash flood events occurred across the Upstate, the most severe flooding was in the Greenville metro area, especially near downtown Greenville (where two fatalities occurred) and in the Greer/Taylors area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol reported flooding at Acorn Dr and Highway 101 (1.5 SSE), likely due to a tributary of Maple Creek overflowing its banks. Public reported water entering homes on Maple Dr, likely due to flooding along Maple Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the a very unstable atmosphere, the presence of a weak surface trough and the passing of a Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) produced numerous showers and thunderstorms. A line of stationary thunderstorms produced between 3-6 inches of rain across the Tri-county area, which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout developed and remained nearly stationary over the river near Waterfront Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an earlier outflow boundary before tracking west-northwest into a highly moist and unstable environment over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in the Habersham area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and very unstable air mass was in place well in advance of an approaching back door cold front. The very unstable air mass combined with numerous boundary interactions over the area resulted in thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee reported nickel size hail near the intersection of Ashley Phosphate Road and Rivers Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front combined with a strongly unstable air mass to produce a large number of large hail reports. There were also several wind damage reports with the worst of the damage around Conway, South Carolina. The severe weather was largely clustered in Horry County, South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms across the Midlands took down trees and some powerlines from microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Post Office reported large branches and several trees down in the north end of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe storms in Lancaster county produced some large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size and a tree down was reported on East Doc Garris Road near Elgin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Summertime pulse thunderstorms developed in the mid to late afternoon hours. These thunderstorms interacted with outflow boundaries and other mesoscale boundaries with a few of the storms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jasper County dispatch office reported one tree down along Rivers Hill Crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms over areas of the northern Pee Dee region produced baseball size hail.  Other areas of the Midlands produced wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported trees down along Bluff Road in the Gadsden and Wateree Communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms produced some wind damage in the eastern Midlands and upper Pee Dee taking down some trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT reported trees down in the Shiloh Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm took down some trees and powerlines causing some power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hwy Dept. reported power outages in Holly Hill and the surrounding area from several trees and powerlines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and some wind damage in the SC CSRA counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported half dollar size hail in New Ellenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed across Upstate South Carolina during the afternoon and evening. Some storms became briefly severe, producing locally large hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee estimated at least 60 mph winds near Tigerville. County comms reported multiple trees and power lines blown down on Bailey Mill Rd and another tree down on Chinquapin rd in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across central and southern portions of the Upstate during the afternoon, with isolated large hail and wind damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: HAM Radio operator reported quarter size hail on Barrett Dr. Public reported quarter size hail on Butler Rd near downtown Mauldin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous strong to severe, discrete thunderstorm cells developed across the Piedmont during the afternoon ahead of a slow-moving, nearly stationary backdoor cold front. The storms were prolific hail producers, with some producing larger than baseball size hail. New cells often developed upstream of mature cells, then moved over the same areas, such that some locations saw two or more rounds of large hail. This was especially true across portions of Spartanburg, Cherokee, Union, and York Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line crossed Upstate South Carolina during the morning hours, knocking down trees and powerlines. Heavy rain also resulted in a few areas of flooding and flash flooding during the course of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Alverson Rd and Oaklawn Rd and power lines were blown down at New Harrison Bridge Rd and Fairview Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line crossed Upstate South Carolina during the morning hours, knocking down trees and powerlines. Heavy rain also resulted in a few areas of flooding and flash flooding during the course of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were blown down across the northern portion of the county.
